suggested_topics: why some bridges last 2000 years and others collapse in 20
how coral reefs build their own weather systems
why birds migrate in V formations — physics not instinct
the hidden math behind musical scales
volcanic glass and its use in prehistoric surgery

formats.name: visual_metaphor
formats.instruction: Describe a real-world issue as if it were an image or scene. Use no more than one vivid paragraph, focusing on sensory detail and mood.

formats.name: micro_manifesto
formats.instruction: Declare an actionable credo or mini-manifesto in three bullet points. Each point should begin with a strong verb and be concise.

formats.name: timeline_glimpse
formats.instruction: Imagine an alternate timeline: briefly outline how things would be different, using 3 dated milestones and brief explanations for each.

formats.name: data_sprint
formats.instruction: Present 2 surprising facts or stats about your chosen topic. Add one interpretive line explaining why they matter. Avoid lengthy context.

formats.name: first_person_flashback
formats.instruction: Recall a specific past moment related to your topic; describe what happened, and how it shaped your thinking. Use 2-3 paragraphs, storytelling style.

formats.name: concept_map
formats.instruction: Lay out a quick conceptual map—describe the topic in three distinct, interconnected branches. Each branch gets 1-2 sentences, no intro.

formats.name: counterexample_challenge
formats.instruction: Take a popular assumption and present a counterexample. Begin with a one-line statement of what most people think, then show your counter and its implications in a follow-up paragraph.

formats.name: haiku_opinion
formats.instruction: Express your take on a topic as a haiku: 3 lines, syllable structure 5-7-5. After the poem, add one sentence for context (optional).

formats.name: annotated_quote
formats.instruction: Pick a relevant quote (by anyone, real or fictional), then write a short annotation explaining what it means in your context and why you chose it.

title_styles: Write a title that juxtaposes two seemingly unrelated ideas. Examples: 'What chess and coffee teach us about routine', 'How gardening fixes code bugs'
Write a poetic or metaphorical title. Examples: 'The echo chamber is a glass house', 'Algorithms as origami'
Write a title that promises a concise takeaway. Examples: 'One thing that changed my workflow forever', 'The two-minute fix for digital clutter'
Write a title in the style of breaking news. Examples: 'Update: Autonomous cars now outsmart potholes', 'Alert: Data privacy gets its day in court'
Write a title that playfully subverts expectations. Examples: 'No, you don’t need another productivity app', 'Why boredom is actually your biggest asset'
structure_variants: Begin with a vivid sensory description, then transition to analysis.
Embed a poll or survey question in the middle of your post.
Open with a hypothetical scenario, then dissect its implications step by step.
Wrap your post with a short, personal anecdote—start and end in first person.
Structure the body as a debate: offer both sides of the argument before giving your conclusion.
topic_angles: Explore the psychology of waiting in lines and crowd behavior.
Investigate the unintended consequences of remote work.
Spotlight the evolution of urban green spaces and their hidden effects.
Analyze the history and resurgence of analog tools (paper, vinyl, film).
Reframe cybersecurity as a social game, not just a technical battle.
